title: Nonreciprocal Landau-Zener Tunneling in the Presence of Heat-Bath-Induced Colored Noise
abstract: In this work, we investigate the effects of heat-bath-induced colored noise on nonreciprocal Landau-Zener tunneling. We perform numerical calculations of the instantaneous energy levels and find that noise induces stochastic fluctuations in these levels. Beyond such fluctuations, sufficiently large nonreciprocity parameters enable noise to further trigger the emergence of multiple exceptional points. The corresponding tunneling probability is significantly modified by colored noise. On the one hand, colored noise generally enhances the tunneling probability under nonadiabatic sweeping. On the other hand, it can break the reciprocity of Landau-Zener tunneling in the adiabatic limit. In particular, for large nonreciprocity parameters, the adiabatic tunneling probability is insensitive to noise and determined by exceptional points. We plot the phase diagram of tunneling probability as a function of the sweep rate and nonreciprocity parameter, for different amplitudes and correlation times of colored noise. Analytically, we derive the exact expression for the tunneling probability in the absence of noise and an approximate solution in the white-noise limit, employing Weber functions and the Wiener-Hermite expansion method. Some implications of our theory are discussed.
summary: The paper explores how colored noise from a thermal bath influences nonreciprocal quantum tunneling. It demonstrates that while noise can break the symmetry of tunneling in certain regimes, the system remains robust against noise in the presence of strong nonreciprocity. This work is significant for understanding how environmental fluctuations affect non-Hermitian quantum dynamics.
main_problem: Investigating how heat-bath-induced colored noise affects nonreciprocal Landau-Zener tunneling and the emergence of exceptional points.
main_result: Colored noise generally enhances tunneling under nonadiabatic sweeping and can break the reciprocity of tunneling in the adiabatic limit, though tunneling remains insensitive to noise in the high-nonreciprocity regime ($\delta > 1$).
method: The study employs the stochastic Schrodinger equation, numerical Runge-Kutta and Heun schemes, and analytical techniques including the Wiener-Hermite expansion and Weber functions.
model_or_system: A non-Hermitian two-level Landau-Zener model coupled to a heat bath modeled via a Drude spectral density, resulting in Ornstein-Uhlenbeck colored noise.
key_observables: Tunneling probability (forward and backward sweeps), ensemble-averaged tunneling probability, and instantaneous energy levels.
important_parameters: Sweep rate ($\alpha$), nonreciprocity parameter ($\delta$), noise amplitude ($D$), and noise correlation rate ($\gamma$).
main_assumptions: Markovian approximation, classical limit ($\hbar 	o 0$), and the use of a steady-state approximation in the high-dissipation regime ($\Gamma \gg 1$).
figures_summary: Phase diagrams of tunneling probability as a function of sweep rate and nonreciprocity parameter for various noise amplitudes and correlation times; comparison of different numerical and analytical solution types.
paper_structure: Introduction of the nonreciprocal LZ model; derivation of the stochastic dynamics and noise model; numerical results for energy levels and tunneling; analytical derivation of tunneling probabilities in the white-noise and high-damping limits; discussion of physical implications.
why_interesting: This paper provides deep insights into the interplay between non-Hermitian physics (exceptional points) and environmental decoherence, which is central to understanding the stability of non-reciprocal quantum devices and open quantum system dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
